summary: Simon Späti argues that data engineering is evolving from traditional data warehousing, driven by the growing need for real-time data, scalable pipelines, and programmatic approaches over drag-and-drop ETL tools. He traces his own journey from BI engineer to data engineering, explaining how the role combines software engineering skills with BI expertise and big data capabilities. The post details how ETL processes, data modeling practices, and even organizational education around data are all transforming. Späti concludes that while data warehouses will always be needed for structured business data, the methods for building them are shifting toward engineering-centric approaches, making data engineers the natural evolution of BI engineers.
key_points: Data engineering combines software engineering, BI engineering, and big data skills - it's the evolution of traditional BI roles as data complexity grows
Python has become the 'Swiss Army Knife' for data engineering, used for data wrangling, ETL, API interactions, and workflow automation with tools like Apache Airflow
Traditional drag-and-drop ETL tools (Informatica, SSIS, DataStage) are being replaced by code-driven, programmatic platforms because 'code is the best abstraction there is for software'
Data modeling is shifting away from strict normalization, surrogate keys, and complex SCDs toward more flexible, collaborative approaches with dynamic schemas
Not every company needs data engineers - the role is most relevant for data-driven products or organizations dealing with high volume, variety, and velocity of data
money_quotes: If English is the language of business, SQL is the language of data and Python the language of engineering.
Code is the best abstraction there is for software rather than using drag and drop tools.
The transformation logic is of a higher need and shouldn't be locked away exclusively for BI developers.
Python is here to stay and it's becoming the Swiss Army Knife of programming languages.
Data engineers at larger companies tend to be more focused on 'enterprise' skills like ETL, BI, and data warehousing, whereas data engineers at smaller companies focus more on core technologies.

In order to get high quality and frequently updated data sets, it is important to distinguish between data pipelines that are done and cleaned by data engineers and all the others that are mostly exploratory.
To be more collaborative and work on same objects it is a necessary trade-off to loosen it up.
key_insight: Data engineering represents the natural evolution of BI engineering as organizations shift from batch-oriented, tool-driven ETL to code-first, real-time data pipelines that require software engineering skills alongside traditional data warehouse knowledge.
